title: Dirichlet L-function
body:
In mathematics, a Dirichlet L-series is a function of the form

  
    
      
        L
        (
        s
        ,
        χ
        )
        =
        
          ∑
          
            n
            =
            1
          
          
            ∞
          
        
        
          
            
              χ
              (
              n
              )
            
            
              n
              
                s
              
            
          
        
        ,
      
    
    {\displaystyle L(s,\chi )=\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac {\chi (n)}{n^{s}}},}
  

where 
  
    
      
        χ
      
    
    {\displaystyle \chi }
  
 is a Dirichlet character and 
  
    
      
        s
      
    
    {\displaystyle s}
  
 a complex variable with real part greater than 
  
    
      
        1
      
    
    {\displaystyle 1}
  
. It is a special case of a Dirichlet series. By analytic continuation, it can be extended to a meromorphic function on the whole complex plane; it is then called a Dirichlet L-function.
These functions are named after Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet who introduced them in 1837 to prove his theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions. In his proof, Dirichlet showed that 
  
    
      
        L
        (
        s
        ,
        χ
        )
      
    
    {\displaystyle L(s,\chi )}
  
 is non-zero at 
  
    
      
        s
        =
        1
      
    
    {\displaystyle s=1}
  
. Moreover, if 
  
    
      
        χ
      
    
    {\displaystyle \chi }
  
 is principal, then the corresponding Dirichlet L-function has a simple pole at 
  
    
      
        s
        =
        1
      
    
    {\displaystyle s=1}
  
. Otherwise, the L-function is entire.
